content:
# Leviticus Chapter 19 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 69r–69v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_019_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_019_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 105–106 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 19 is the theological and ethical apex of the Holiness Code and, by many accounts, of the entire Torah's social legislation. It opens with the governing formula of the whole code: "You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy" (v.2) — qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem. What follows is one of the most remarkable documents of ancient moral reasoning ever produced: a mosaic of commandments that moves without apparent system across cultic, agricultural, commercial, legal, and interpersonal domains, but whose governing logic becomes clear only when the frame is seen. The chapter climaxes twice — first at v.18b, ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha, "you shall love your neighbor as yourself," the verse Jesus and the rabbis both identified as the heart of the Torah; and second at v.34, extending that same love to the stranger/sojourner, ve-ahavta lo kamokha, "you shall love him as yourself." The chapter's literary structure has been variously analyzed as a decalogic series (the phrase "I am the LORD" or "I am the LORD your God" appears eighteen times as a refrain), or as a thematic ring composition, or as a loose anthology unified only by the Holiness formula. Whatever its structure, its content is extraordinary: parents, sabbath, idolatry (vv.3–4); sacrifice integrity (vv.5–8); agricultural gleaning for the poor (vv.9–10); theft, lying, and oaths (vv.11–12); oppression of workers and the vulnerable (vv.13–14); just judgment and slander (vv.15–16); hatred, rebuke, and vengeance (vv.17–18a); the great commandment (v.18b); laws of mixing (v.19); laws concerning a slave woman pledged to another (vv.20–22); first-fruits of trees (vv.23–25); blood, divination, mourning rites, and cultic practices (vv.26–31); honor to the aged (v.32); love the sojourner (vv.33–34); honest weights and measures (vv.35–36); summary (v.37). The chapter's ethical vision — that holiness is expressed not in ritual purity alone but in economic fairness, honest speech, impartial justice, and love of the vulnerable — was foundational to the prophets, to Second Temple Judaism, and to the New Testament.]*

---

## Leviticus 19

**19:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**19:2** "Speak to all the congregation of the sons of Yisra'el and say to them: You shall be holy [qedoshim tihyu — קְדֹשִׁים תִּהְיוּ], for I — the LORD your God — am holy [qadosh — קָדוֹשׁ].

**19:3** Each man his mother and his father you shall revere [tira'u — תִּירָאוּ], and my sabbaths you shall keep. I am the LORD your God.

**19:4** Do not turn to the idols [ha-elilim — הָאֱלִילִים], and molten gods [elohei massekah — אֱלֹהֵי מַסֵּכָה] you shall not make for yourselves. I am the LORD your God.

**19:5** And when you slaughter a sacrifice of peace-offerings [zevach shelamim — זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים] to the LORD, you shall slaughter it for your acceptance [liretzonkhem — לִרְצֹנְכֶם].

**19:6** On the day of your slaughtering it shall be eaten, and on the morrow; and what remains until the third day — in fire it shall be burned.

**19:7** And if it is eaten at all on the third day — it is a putrid thing [pigul — פִּגּוּל] it is; it will not be accepted.

**19:8** And the one who eats it shall bear his iniquity, for the holy thing of the LORD he has profaned; and that person shall be cut off [ve-nikhretah — וְנִכְרְתָה] from among his people.

**19:9** And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not complete the corner [pe'at — פְּאַת] of your field in your reaping, and the gleaning [leqet — לֶקֶט] of your harvest you shall not gather.

**19:10** And your vineyard you shall not go over a second time [lo te'olel — לֹא תְעוֹלֵל], and the fallen grapes [peret — פֶּרֶט] of your vineyard you shall not gather; for the poor [le-ani — לֶעָנִי] and for the sojourner [ve-la-ger — וְלַגֵּר] you shall leave them. I am the LORD your God.

**19:11** You shall not steal, and you shall not deny falsely [lo tekachashу — לֹא תְכַחֲשׁוּ], and you shall not lie [lo teshaqru — לֹא תְשַׁקְּרוּ] one to his neighbor.

**19:12** And you shall not swear by my name to falsehood, and you shall profane [ve-chillalta — וְחִלַּלְתָּ] the name of your God. I am the LORD.

**19:13** You shall not oppress [lo ta'ashoq — לֹא תַעֲשֹׁק] your neighbor and you shall not rob [lo tigzol — לֹא תִגְזֹל]; the wages of a hired man [sekhar sakir — שְׂכַר שָׂכִיר] shall not remain with you overnight until morning.

**19:14** You shall not curse [lo teqallel — לֹא תְקַלֵּל] the deaf [cheresh — חֵרֵשׁ], and before the blind [iver — עִוֵּר] you shall not put a stumbling block [mikhshol — מִכְשֹׁל]; and you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.

**19:15** You shall not do injustice [lo ta'asu avel — לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ עָוֶל] in judgment; you shall not show partiality to the poor [lo tissa feni dal — לֹא תִשָּׂא פְנֵי דָל] and you shall not honor the great [lo tahdar pene gadol — לֹא תֶהְדַּר פְּנֵי גָדוֹל]; in righteousness [be-tsedeq — בְּצֶדֶק] you shall judge your neighbor.

**19:16** You shall not go as a slanderer [lo telekh rakhil — לֹא תֵלֵךְ רָכִיל] among your people; you shall not stand against the blood [lo ta'amod al dam — לֹא תַעֲמֹד עַל דַּם] of your neighbor. I am the LORD.

**19:17** You shall not hate [lo tisna — לֹא תִשְׂנָא] your brother in your heart; you shall surely rebuke [hokhei'ach tokhiach — הוֹכֵחַ תּוֹכִיחַ] your neighbor and you shall not bear sin because of him.

**19:18** You shall not take vengeance [lo tiqom — לֹא תִקֹּם] and you shall not bear a grudge [lo titor — לֹא תִטֹּר] against the sons of your people; and you shall love your neighbor as yourself [ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha — וְאָהַבְתָּ לְרֵעֲךָ כָּמוֹךָ]. I am the LORD.

**19:19** My statutes you shall keep: your cattle you shall not cause to mate with a different kind [kil'ayim — כִּלְאַיִם]; your field you shall not sow with two kinds; and a garment of mixed-stuff [sha'atnez — שַׁעַטְנֵז], two kinds, shall not come upon you.

**19:20** And a man who lies with a woman — lying with seed [shikevat zera' — שִׁכְבַת זֶרַע] — and she is a slave-woman [shifchah — שִׁפְחָה] pledged [necharefet — נֶחֱרֶפֶת] to a man, and redeeming she has not been redeemed nor freedom given to her — an inquiry shall be made [biqoret tihyeh — בִּקֹּרֶת תִּהְיֶה]; they shall not be put to death, because she was not free.

**19:21** And he shall bring his guilt-offering [asham — אָשָׁם] to the LORD, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — a ram for a guilt-offering.

**19:22** And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt-offering before the LORD for his sin that he sinned; and he shall be forgiven for his sin that he sinned.

**19:23** And when you come into the land and plant any tree for food, you shall treat its fruit as forbidden [va-araltem orlato — וַעֲרַלְתֶּם עׇרְלָתוֹ]: three years it shall be treated as uncircumcised [arelim — עֲרֵלִים] for you; it shall not be eaten.

**19:24** And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy [qodesh — קֹדֶשׁ], a praise-offering [hillulim — הִלּוּלִים] to the LORD.

**19:25** And in the fifth year you shall eat its fruit, for you to add its produce [le-hosif lakhem tevuato — לְהוֹסִיף לָכֶם תְּבוּאָתוֹ]. I am the LORD your God.

**19:26** You shall not eat upon the blood [al ha-dam — עַל הַדָּם]. You shall not practice divination [lo tenachashи — לֹא תְנַחֲשׁוּ] and you shall not practice augury [lo te'onenu — לֹא תְעוֹנֵנוּ].

**19:27** You shall not round off [lo taqifu — לֹא תַקִּיפוּ] the corner [pe'at — פְּאַת] of your head, and you shall not destroy [lo tashchit — לֹא תַשְׁחִית] the corner [pe'at — פְּאַת] of your beard.

**19:28** And a cutting for the dead [seret la-nefesh — שֶׂרֶט לָנֶפֶשׁ] you shall not make in your flesh, and tattoo writing [ketovet qa'aqa' — כְּתֹבֶת קַעֲקַע] you shall not put on yourselves. I am the LORD.

**19:29** Do not profane [al techallel — אַל תְּחַלֵּל] your daughter by making her a prostitute [le-haznota — לְהַזְנוֹתָהּ], lest the land fall to prostitution and the land become full of depravity [zimmah — זִמָּה].

**19:30** My sabbaths you shall keep, and my sanctuary you shall revere. I am the LORD.

**19:31** Do not turn to the mediums [ha-ovot — הָאֹבוֹת] and to the spirits of the dead [yid'onim — הַיִּדְּעֹנִים] — do not seek them out to be defiled by them. I am the LORD your God.

**19:32** Before a gray head [seva — שֵׂיבָה] you shall rise, and you shall honor the face of an elder [zaqen — זָקֵן], and you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.

**19:33** And when a sojourner [ger — גֵּר] sojourns with you in your land, you shall not oppress [lo tonu — לֹא תוֹנוּ] him.

**19:34** As a native among you [ke-ezrach mikkem — כְּאֶזְרָח מִכֶּם] shall the sojourner who sojourns with you be to you, and you shall love him as yourself [ve-ahavta lo kamokha — וְאָהַבְתָּ לוֹ כָּמוֹךָ], for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

**19:35** You shall not do injustice in judgment, in measure [ba-midah — בַּמִּדָּה], in weight [ba-mishqal — בַּמִּשְׁקָל], or in liquid measure [ba-meshurah — בַּמְּשׁוּרָה].

**19:36** Balances of righteousness [mozne tsedeq — מֹאזְנֵי צֶדֶק], stones of righteousness [avne tsedeq — אַבְנֵי צֶדֶק], an ephah of righteousness [eifat tsedeq — אֵיפַת צֶדֶק], and a hin of righteousness [hin tsedeq — הִין צֶדֶק] — shall be to you. I am the LORD your God who brought you out from the land of Egypt.

**19:37** And you shall keep all my statutes and all my judgments, and you shall do them. I am the LORD."

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Overview

Chapter 19 is the ethical and theological summit of the Holiness Code (Lev 17–26) and one of the most compositionally dense chapters in the Torah. Its governing formula — "I am the LORD" (ani YHWH) or "I am the LORD your God" (ani YHWH Eloheikhem) — appears no fewer than eighteen times, functioning as both a legal sanction and a theological grounding: obedience to these laws flows from the identity of the Lawgiver, not merely from coercive enforcement. The chapter's internal logic moves between what might loosely be called "ritual" and "ethical" domains, but this dichotomy would have been meaningless to the ancient Israelite mind: honest weights and honest speech are as much acts of holiness as sabbath observance. The famous command ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha (v.18b) appears not as a free-standing maxim but as the culmination of a tightly argued sequence on hatred, vengeance, and bearing grudges (vv.17–18a). Similarly, the extension of that same love to the ger (sojourner) in v.34 — ve-ahavta lo kamokha — grounds the ethics of stranger-love not in abstract universalism but in historical memory: "for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt."

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v.2 — qedoshim tihyu:** The plural imperative addressed to "all the congregation" (kol adat — an unusual addressee; Torah commands are typically addressed to individuals or to leaders). The quality of qedushah (holiness) here is not merely ritual separation but ethical differentiation from the nations. The ground of the command — "for I am holy" — is theologically audacious: Israel is called to imitate a divine attribute.

**v.3 — mother before father:** Note the inverted order from the Decalogue (Exod 20:12: "Honor your father and your mother"). Rabbinic interpretation suggested the inversion was deliberate: a child naturally fears the father (authority figure) and loves the mother, so the text corrects the natural inclination by demanding reverence for the mother first. The Hebrew tira'u — "you shall revere/fear" — is stronger than the Decalogue's "honor" (kabed). The pairing of parents (v.3a) with sabbath (v.3b) echoes the Decalogue structure (Exod 20:8–12), grounding both in the same holiness demand.

**v.4 — ha-elilim:** "The idols" — a term with a probable overtone of "nothings" or "worthless things" (from the root 'al or 'elil, "nothing"). The term occurs frequently in prophetic polemic (Isa 2:8, 18; 10:10–11; Ezek 30:13). The molten gods (elohei massekah) were likely cast-metal cult images. The prohibition pairs negatively with the positive holiness call of v.2.

**v.5 — liretzonkhem:** "For your acceptance" — this is the technical sacrificial term for a valid, accepted offering. The peace-offering (shelamim) was the communal sacrificial meal; the law of vv.5–8 regulates how long the meat remains valid for eating. After two days any remainder must be burned; consuming it on the third day makes it pigul — a term denoting sacral invalidity, the offering having "putrefied" in ritual terms.

**v.9–10 — pe'ah and leqet:** The law of the corner (pe'ah) and gleaning (leqet) is among the most celebrated pieces of Israelite agrarian legislation and the legal basis of the story of Ruth. Pe'ah designates the corner or edge of the field left unharvested; the amount is not specified here (rabbinic law set a minimum of one-sixtieth). Leqet (gleaning) are the stalks dropped during reaping. The vineyard equivalent is ole'lot (unripe clusters left on the vine, v.10a) and peret (individual fallen grapes). Both pe'ah and leqet are designated for the 'ani (poor, economically vulnerable) and the ger (resident alien/sojourner). The pairing of poor and sojourner is characteristic of the Holiness Code's social vision.

**v.11 — three prohibitions in sequence:** Lo tignovu ("you shall not steal"), lo tekachashу ("you shall not deny/deceive"), lo teshaqru ("you shall not lie"). The sequence moves from act (theft) to verbal denial of the act (lying about it) to false oath (v.12). This is a graduated treatment of deception.

**v.12 — profaning the Name:** The prohibition against swearing falsely by the divine name connects the ethical and cultic: a false oath desecrates (chillel) YHWH's name, appropriating divine authority for human deception. This verse is the legal ground of the Third Commandment ("You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain," Exod 20:7).

**v.13 — sekhar sakir lo talin:** "The wages of a hired man shall not remain with you overnight." The hired laborer (sakir) lives on daily wages; to withhold them even one night is a form of oppression (oshek). The same law appears in Deut 24:14–15 with the emotional intensification: "for he is poor and his life depends on it." This verse was the basis of later Jewish labor law requiring same-day payment for day laborers.

**v.14 — cheresh and iver:** The deaf and blind are paradigmatic of the vulnerable who cannot defend themselves against exploitation. Cursing the deaf — who cannot hear the curse and respond — and placing a stumbling block before the blind — who cannot see it and avoid it — represent the abuse of power over those unable to retaliate. The phrase "you shall fear your God" (ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha) serves as the enforcement sanction where human detection is impossible: only God sees what is done to those who cannot witness against their abusers.

**v.15 — tsedeq in judgment:** The two prohibitions flank the positive command: do not favor the poor (lo tissa feni dal) and do not honor the great (lo tahdar pene gadol) — flanking be-tsedeq tishpot amitekha, "in righteousness you shall judge your fellow." Both favoritism and deference are violations of judicial integrity. The poor do not automatically win cases; the powerful do not automatically lose them. Justice is person-blind.

**v.16 — rakhil and dam:** Lo telekh rakhil — "you shall not go as a slanderer" — is literally "you shall not walk as a trader/peddler [of words]." The rakhil is the gossip who travels between parties spreading harmful information. Lo ta'amod al dam re'akha — "you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor" — is disputed: it may mean "do not stand by while your neighbor's life is endangered" (the passive reading, requiring active rescue), or "do not testify to shed blood" (the active reading, prohibiting false witness in capital cases). The active rescue interpretation has dominated in rabbinic jurisprudence (b. Sanhedrin 73a).

**v.17 — hokhei'ach tokhiach:** The absolute infinitive construction demands strong translation: "you shall surely rebuke" or "rebuke you must rebuke." This is one of only a few places in the Torah where active confrontation is commanded rather than passive avoidance of sin. The command to rebuke connects directly to the prohibition on hate: harboring silent hatred (lo tisna et achicha bi-levavekha — "do not hate your brother in your heart") is addressed by requiring open rebuke rather than hidden resentment. "You shall not bear sin because of him" — the failure to rebuke makes the silent witness share in the guilt.

**v.18 — lo tiqom / lo titor:** Two precise legal distinctions. Tiqom (vengeance) is retaliatory harm for harm received. Titor (bearing a grudge) is withholding reciprocal benefit as indirect retaliation ("I won't lend you my ox because you wouldn't lend me yours"). The Sifra illustrates both. The culmination — ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha — does not stand alone but is the resolution of the entire sequence: vengeance and grudge-bearing are displaced by love. Kamokha ("as yourself") means: your neighbor's interests are as real and urgent to you as your own. Rabbi Akiva called this "the greatest principle in the Torah" (klal gadol ba-Torah — j. Nedarim 9:4). Jesus cited it as the second commandment equal to the love of God (Mark 12:31).

**v.19 — kil'ayim:** The prohibition on mixing (kil'ayim — often translated "mixed kinds" but the etymology is obscure; perhaps from the root kala', "to withhold/restrain") covers three domains: animal breeding, agricultural sowing, and textile weaving. The garment prohibition (sha'atnez — wool and linen woven together) is elaborated in Deut 22:11. The theological rationale is never given; rabbinic tradition classified these as chuqqim (statutes without explicit reason), and modern interpreters have proposed various explanations (creation-order preservation; priestly exemption from sha'atnez). None is textually confirmed.

**v.20–22 — the slave-woman law:** This is one of the most legally technical and morally difficult passages in the chapter. A slave-woman (shifchah) "pledged" (necharefet — the root chrf suggests designation or assignment) to a man — meaning she has been betrothed but the betrothal is incomplete because she has not yet been redeemed from slavery or given her freedom. Sexual intercourse with her constitutes a violation, but the death penalty (normally for adultery) does not apply because she is not free. The man must bring a guilt-offering (asham). The passage reflects the specific legal vulnerability of enslaved women in antiquity while also placing a legal obligation on the perpetrating free man — a significant limitation compared to surrounding ancient Near Eastern law.

**v.23–25 — orlah:** The fruit of a newly planted tree is called its orlah (literally "foreskin") — uncircumcised, forbidden. For three years it is prohibited entirely; in the fourth year it is sanctified as hillulim (a praise-offering, from hallel); only in the fifth year may the owner eat freely. The law functions as a kind of first-fruits dedication applied to arboriculture and embeds agricultural patience within the sanctification framework of the Holiness Code.

**v.26 — al ha-dam:** "You shall not eat upon the blood" is interpreted variously: (1) the prohibition against eating meat before the blood has drained (cf. Gen 9:4; Lev 17:10–14); (2) a prohibition against the pagan practice of eating beside/over a sacrificial pool of blood (a divination or omen-reading practice). The immediate pairing with nachash (divination by omens) and 'onen (augury/cloud-reading) suggests the cultic-magical context is primary here.

**v.27 — pe'at ha-rosh / pe'at zeqan:** The prohibition on "rounding the corner of the head" and "destroying the corner of the beard" prohibits specific mourning or cultic haircuts associated with pagan practice. The exact technique is uncertain; the phrase pe'at likely refers to the temporal region and the sideburn/cheek area. Later Jewish practice derived from this verse the requirement to leave payot (sidelocks).

**v.28 — seret la-nefesh / ketovet qa'aqa':** Cutting the flesh for the dead (cf. Deut 14:1; Jer 16:6; 41:5) and tattooing were both associated with mourning rites and possibly with cultic identification marks for the dead or for pagan deities. The phrase ketovet qa'aqa' is the only occurrence of qa'aqa' in the Hebrew Bible; it clearly refers to incised marking of the skin.

**v.29 — al techallel:** The prohibition on making one's daughter a prostitute (le-haznota — Hiphil infinitive of zanah) for financial gain. The motivation given is communal: prostitution desecrates (chillel) the social fabric and fills the land with zimmah (depravity, licentiousness). This verse may also address sacred prostitution (cf. Deut 23:18), though the text does not specify.

**v.31 — ha-ovot ve-ha-yid'onim:** The mediums (ovot — a term linked to leather bottles or hollow vessels through which spirits spoke) and "knowers" (yid'onim — those with familiar spirits). These are necromantic practices — the attempt to consult the dead for guidance (cf. the witch of En-dor, 1 Sam 28). The prohibition is on "turning to" (lo tifnu) and "seeking after" (ve-lo tidreshу) them — both verbs normally used of seeking divine guidance, suggesting these practices represent a rival form of divination to legitimate Yahwistic prophecy.

**v.32 — seva / zaqen:** "Before a gray head you shall rise" — a concrete posture of honor requiring physical action (standing). The pairing of seva (grayness, denoting age) and zaqen (elder, with associated dignity and authority) grounds the honor command both in natural fact (aging) and social role. The grounding phrase is again "you shall fear your God" — the aged person may not be able to enforce respect; God witnesses what the young person does when the elder enters.

**v.33–34 — the great commandment extended:** The word ger (sojourner, resident alien, stranger dwelling among Israelites) is not a tourist but a permanent or semi-permanent resident without full legal standing. The command is first negative (lo tonu — do not oppress/wrong) and then positive: ke-ezrach mikkem ("as the native among you") shall the sojourner be — identical legal standing. The positive love-command repeats the syntax of v.18b: ve-ahavta lo kamokha, "you shall love him as yourself." The grounding is historical empathy: "for sojourners were you in the land of Egypt." This is the twelfth time in the Torah that Israel's sojourn in Egypt is cited as the ground for treating the stranger well (cf. Exod 22:20; 23:9; Deut 10:19).

**v.35–36 — honest measures:** The sequence of measure (midah), weight (mishqal), and liquid measure (meshurah) covers all basic commercial transactions. The fourfold "righteousness" (tsedeq) — balances of righteousness, stones of righteousness, ephah of righteousness, hin of righteousness — applies the same word used for judicial integrity (v.15) to the marketplace. Commercial fraud is as much a violation of tsedeq as judicial partiality. The ephah was a dry-grain measure (roughly 22 liters); the hin was a liquid measure (roughly 3.7 liters). The stone weights were the standard counterweights used on balance scales.

**v.37 — summary:** The closing formula echoes the opening of the Sinai covenant (cf. Exod 20:1–2) and frames the entire chapter as covenant law: "you shall keep all my statutes and all my judgments, and you shall do them." The double object — chuqqotai (statutes, which may not be rationally explained) and mishpatai (judgments, which reflect moral reason) — encompasses the full range of divine command.

---

## Key Term Table

| Hebrew | Transliteration | EMET Rendering | Notes |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| קְדֹשִׁים תִּהְיוּ | qedoshim tihyu | "you shall be holy" | Governing imperative of the Holiness Code |
| גֵּר | ger | "sojourner" | Resident alien without full legal standing |
| פְּאָה | pe'ah | "corner" | Agricultural edge left unharvested for the poor |
| לֶקֶט | leqet | "gleaning" | Fallen stalks or grapes left for the poor |
| עָוֶל | avel | "injustice" | Judicial/commercial wrongdoing |
| צֶדֶק | tsedeq | "righteousness" | Judicial and commercial integrity |
| רָכִיל | rakhil | "slanderer" | Literally "peddler [of words]"; gossip/informer |
| הוֹכֵחַ תּוֹכִיחַ | hokhei'ach tokhiach | "you shall surely rebuke" | Absolute infinitive: mandatory confrontation |
| וְאָהַבְתָּ לְרֵעֲךָ כָּמוֹךָ | ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha | "you shall love your neighbor as yourself" | The great commandment; v.18b |
| כִּלְאַיִם | kil'ayim | "mixed kinds" | Prohibition on mixing species/fibers |
| שַׁעַטְנֵז | sha'atnez | "mixed-stuff" | Wool-linen garment blend; foreign term |
| עׇרְלָה | orlah | "uncircumcision" | Fruit of newly planted tree; forbidden three years |
| הִלּוּלִים | hillulim | "praise-offering" | Fourth-year fruit dedicated to the LORD |
| אֹבוֹת | ovot | "mediums" | Necromantic practitioners |
| יִדְּעֹנִים | yid'onim | "spirits of the dead" | "Knowers"; those with familiar spirits |
| שֵׂיבָה | seva | "gray head" | Gray hair as emblem of old age deserving honor |
| כְּאֶזְרָח מִכֶּם | ke-ezrach mikkem | "as the native among you" | Full legal equivalence for sojourner |
| מֹאזְנֵי צֶדֶק | mozne tsedeq | "balances of righteousness" | Honest scales; commercial integrity |
| אֵיפַת צֶדֶק | eifat tsedeq | "ephah of righteousness" | Standard dry measure (~22 liters) |
| הִין צֶדֶק | hin tsedeq | "hin of righteousness" | Standard liquid measure (~3.7 liters) |
| זִמָּה | zimmah | "depravity" | Licentiousness; moral corruption of the land |
| פִּגּוּל | pigul | "putrid thing" | Sacrificial meat rendered invalid |
| שֶׂרֶט לָנֶפֶשׁ | seret la-nefesh | "cutting for the dead" | Pagan mourning incision |
| כְּתֹבֶת קַעֲקַע | ketovet qa'aqa' | "tattoo writing" | Only occurrence of qa'aqa' in HB |

---

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 105–106)

**v.2:** LXX reads *hagioi esesthe* (ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε) — "you shall be holy" — matching MT exactly in semantic force. The Greek *hagios* (holy, set apart) renders Hebrew *qadosh* throughout the Holiness Code consistently.

**v.13:** LXX *ou me apostereses* — "you shall not defraud/withhold" — for Hebrew *lo tashchot*; Vaticanus reads more precisely than some LXX traditions here.

**v.16:** LXX renders *rakhil* as *eidolon* in some traditions, but Vaticanus appears to read *sykophantes* (slanderer/informer), which better captures the forensic nuance of the Hebrew term.

**v.17:** LXX *elenchon elenxeis* — matching the absolute infinitive force of *hokhei'ach tokhiach* precisely. This is notable; the LXX translators recognized the emphatic construction and reproduced it with a cognate infinitive in Greek, a relatively rare Greek construction deployed specifically to match the Hebrew intensification.

**v.18:** The great commandment in LXX: *kai agapeseis ton plesion sou hos seauton* (καὶ ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν) — "you shall love your neighbor as yourself." This is the precise form quoted by Jesus in Mark 12:31, Matthew 22:39, and by Paul in Romans 13:9 and Galatians 5:14. The LXX rendering of *re'a* as *plesion* (neighbor, one nearby) slightly narrows the Hebrew, which can include a wider range of social relations.

**v.19:** LXX treats *sha'atnez* as a foreign loanword and either transliterates or renders it as *kibdelos* (counterfeit/mixed material). The MT term *sha'atnez* appears to be a non-Hebrew loan word of uncertain origin (Egyptian? Nubian?), and the LXX translators clearly did not know its precise meaning.

**v.23:** LXX renders *arelim* (uncircumcised) as *akatharta* (unclean) — a significant interpretive shift. Where MT uses the circumcision metaphor for forbidden fruit, LXX substitutes the purity framework. This is a consistent pattern in the LXX: the ritual-body metaphors of circumcision are often replaced by the purity vocabulary more familiar to Greek readers.

**v.29:** LXX *ou bebelesseis* — "you shall not profane" — matches MT *al techallel* precisely. The LXX *bebeloo* (to profane, desecrate) is the standard rendering of Hebrew *chillel* in sacral contexts.

**v.34:** LXX extends the neighbor-love commandment to the stranger with *kai agapeseis auton hos seauton* — "you shall love him as yourself" — parallel to v.18 and equally precise. The LXX is careful to preserve both instances of the kamokha ("as yourself") construction, both for neighbor (v.18) and sojourner (v.34).

**v.36:** LXX *zugos dikaios, stathmes dikaiai* — "righteous balance, righteous weights" — rendering the Hebrew *mozne tsedeq, avne tsedeq* literally. The Greek *dikaios* (righteous, just) renders *tsedeq* consistently throughout the chapter, confirming the Greek translators understood the commercial-justice domain of *tsedeq* alongside its judicial domain.

---

*EMET translation completed. Leviticus 19:1–37. All 37 verses rendered. Source: Leningrad Codex folios 69r–69v. LXX: Vaticanus pp. 105–106.*
